elements.sent-2411-001: Functional safety forms an important aspect in the design of systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Its emphasis on the automotive industry has evolved significantly over the years.
elements.sent-2411-003: Till date many methods have been developed to get appropriate FTA(Fault Tree analysis) for various scenarios and features pertaining to Autonomous Driving.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper is an attempt to explore the scope of using Generative Artificial Intelligence(GenAI) in order to develop Fault Tree Analysis(FTA) with the use case of malfunction for the Lidar sensor in mind.
elements.sent-2411-005: We explore various available open source Large Language Models(LLM) models and then dive deep into one of them to study its responses and provide our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-006: This paper successfully shows the possibility to train existing Large Language models through Prompt Engineering for fault tree analysis for any Autonomy usecase aided with PlantUML tool.
elements.sent-2411-007: According to \cite{KAPITONOV2022556 , it is mandatory to ensure safety into the products put into circulation in any country.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Safety is more so relevant today due to the amount on equipment installed on the vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-009: Particularly in the Autonomy usecase, we have innumerable sensors like Lidar, Radar, Camera and others necessary to enable the self-driving features of the vehicle in consideration.
elements.sent-2411-010: The two most important standards which come to the mind when we think about safety of Automotive is ISO 26262(Functional Safety of Road Vehicles)\cite{pathak2024iso and PAS-21448(Safety Of The Intended Functionality)\cite{schnellbach2019development .}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: FuSa(Functional Safety) and SOTIF(Safety Of The Intended Functionality)
elements.sent-2411-011: Algorithms are developed that can make decisions in real-time by weighing the potential outcomes of different actions.
elements.sent-2411-012: These algorithms must be transparent and understandable to ensure public trust.
elements.sent-2411-013: It is necessary to establish transparent and clear policies for how autonomous vehicles should behave in different scenarios and ODD (Operational Design Domain)\cite{automated2020avsc conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: This can be then communicated to the public to build trust and acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-015: One approach can be to combine FuSa and SOTIF principles to create a multi-layered safety approach that ensures both the reliability of the vehicle's systems and the safety of its intended functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-016: Data from real-world driving and simulations can be used to continually improve the decision-making algorithms, ensuring they remain effective as new ethical challenges and scenarios emerge.
elements.sent-2411-017: For FuSa and SOTIF to be merged and safety use cases to be created, the GenAI based approaches can be leveraged to come up with different analysis at different stages of FuSa/SOTIF HW/SW development.
elements.sent-2411-018: Generative Artificial Intelligence(GenAI) is a concept that has evolved from Artificial Intelligence\cite{10463265 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Earlier there was the advent of neural networks\cite{gurney2018introduction which were built from basic mathematical principles of backpropagation [\cite{rojas1996backpropagation ].}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Overtime these neural networks were used to train Machine Learning models for prediction algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-021: Multiple use cases of this were to study crop withering[\cite{nyarukowa2016swapdt ], object detection for basic self driving features[\cite{simhambhatla2019self ] and so on.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Eventually Natural Language Processing gained prominence and paved the way for the advent of Large Language Models\cite{chowdhary2020natural .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The popular examples for these models today are OpenAI's ChatGPT-4 and Google's Gemini.
elements.sent-2411-024: Its common place now to utilize these technologies to leverage code optimizations and do other redundant work like framing emails and generating AI images.
elements.sent-2411-025: GenAI in a nutshell can be defined as technology focused on creating new data often resembling existing data.
elements.sent-2411-026: Some common types of this technology are elaborated below\ref{Common models in Generative AI domain : 1.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Generative Adversarial Networks(GANs) This has two neural networks in its design: Generator and a Discriminator.
elements.sent-2411-028: Generator's job is to create new data and the discriminator, as the name suggests tries to distinguish real data from Generator's creation The output of this combination will give the desired creative result.
elements.sent-2411-029: This is majorly used in creating product designs, composing music and developing novel materials.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-030: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) VAEs also work with data.
elements.sent-2411-031: It used encoding/decoding techniques to generate new data.
elements.sent-2411-032: It has a neural network which compresses the training data when encoding and then decodes it back to reconstruct it.VAE introduces randomness when decoding.
elements.sent-2411-033: This helps to create variable outputs which are similar to the input data.
elements.sent-2411-034: This is majorly used to create variations in existing artwork, generate realistic 3D models and anomaly detection.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-035: Autoregressive models These ML models generate data sequentially, predicting the next element based on the previously generated elements.
elements.sent-2411-036: This can be text, code or any other sequential data type.
elements.sent-2411-037: This is majorly used in creative writing, chatbot responses and music composition.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-038: Transformer-based models These are another set of powerful neural network based models which deal with sequential data.
elements.sent-2411-039: These are used for generative tasks by conditioning the model on a specific point or a theme to generate new, relevant data This is majorly used in generating creative text formats like poems and scripts, translating languages and summarizing text content.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-040: Reinforcement learning for Generative Tasks Here a reinforcement learning agent interacts with the surrounding environment and receives good feedback for generating desired types of data.
elements.sent-2411-041: Over time, the agent learns to create increasingly better outputs based on the reward feedback.
elements.sent-2411-042: This has major applications in robotics, design game levels and autonomous systems.
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=3.5in]{GenAI_model.PNG}} \caption{Common models in Generative AI domain} \label{Common models in Generative AI domain}
elements.sent-2411-043: Hazard and Operability Study originally developed as an engineering tool utilized in the process industries to prevent occurrence of industrial hazards and plan mitigation strategies\cite{10124508 .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Overtime, its applications increased in various industries including identifying risk and hazards in engineering systems.
elements.sent-2411-045: The focus of HAZOP is mainly to avoid workplace hazards.
elements.sent-2411-046: But we will be leveraging the contents given in the HAZOP process to effectively consider a usecase in Autonomous driving and perform safety analysis for the same.
elements.sent-2411-047: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) \cite{European_Standards is a process that identifies potential failures with assets and other areas of business.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The benefits of utilizing FMEA include reducing potential failures, saving lives, and lowering excessive costs.
elements.sent-2411-049: FMEA analysis is out of scope for the current paper and will be elaborated in the next research paper.
elements.sent-2411-050: Proactive Risk Identification: FTA helps pinpoint potential failures before they occur, preventing costly recalls and reputational damage.
elements.sent-2411-051: Improved Safety: By identifying hazards and their root causes, OEMs can implement measures to enhance vehicle safety.
elements.sent-2411-052: Enhanced Reliability: FTA contributes to designing and manufacturing vehicles that are less likely to break down, leading to increased customer satisfaction.
elements.sent-2411-053: Regulatory Compliance: FTA can help ensure that vehicles meet safety standards and regulations, avoiding fines and legal issues.
elements.sent-2411-054: Cost Reduction: Identifying potential failures early can save money by preventing costly repairs and downtime.
elements.sent-2411-055: Optimized Design and Manufacturing: FTA can inform design decisions and manufacturing processes, leading to more reliable and efficient vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-056: Fault Tree Analysis is a top-down approach originally developed in Bell laboratories by H.. Watson and A.
elements.sent-2411-057: Mearns for the Air Force in 1962\cite{10124508 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: This concept was later adopted by Boeing, and today, it is widely used in the aerospace, automobile, chemical, nuclear, and software industries, especially for reliability and safety events.
elements.sent-2411-059: FTA (within HAZOP context)\cite{HarnessingAI Definition: Within a HAZOP framework, FTA is used to provide a structured, graphical representation of the relationships between different failures and their potential causes.}
elements.sent-2411-060: It can help in understanding the root causes of hazards identified in the HAZOP study and in evaluating the probability of different combinations of failures leading to an undesirable event.
elements.sent-2411-061: Application: FTA can be employed after a HAZOP study to analyze complex interactions and dependencies between different failures, helping to identify critical points of failure and potential mitigation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-062: Table\ref{A-general-comparison-between-FTA-FMEA-and-HAZOP-12 shows a list of basic questions one can ask to determine the work involved and its dependencies when it comes to FTA, FMEA and HAZOP.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Initial methods for effective FTA analysis have been illustrated in \cite{campean2008function .}
elements.sent-2411-064: This standard describes in detail the required risk analysis and evaluation procedure and safety measures, while appropriately reflecting on the system design definition.
elements.sent-2411-065: Therefore, much research has been done on the risk analysis procedure, wherein the design information is mostly extracted from physical components of similar systems already in operation, the information traced back to obtain constituent functions, and then methods of identifying risk sources are studied.
elements.sent-2411-066: The paper has good conceptual design work done.
elements.sent-2411-067: Eventually its the job of safety engineers to leverage this to perform the fault tree analysis and risk analysis of the system in consideration.
elements.sent-2411-068: Automotive Airbag system is the first non-classical feature of modern cars which complements the safety systems that protect the occupants of the vehicle.Airbags are majorly to protect in a crash situation, however may result in adverse effects if inadvertently deployed \cite{swarup2014safety .This paper performs FTA analysis on the airbag system to identify the various faults that can lead to a top level undesired event, leading to an accident.}
elements.sent-2411-069: This helps in getting early maturity of the airbag design in aspects of safety.
elements.sent-2411-070: Coming to GenAI, \cite{silvis2024sart focuses on the safety analysis of radiation therapy(RT), an effective technique to treat cancer.}
elements.sent-2411-071: It involves complex machines, human operators and software which make it safety critical.
elements.sent-2411-072: Although the functionality is exclusive to Automotive, it has similar issues which are observed in safety analysis of Automotive vehicles: varying FMEA and FTA across RT Organizations.
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus i-SART , a GenAI based algorithms developed using NLP(Natural Language Processing) techniques is developed to promote global implementation of safe RT practices.
elements.sent-2411-074: A recent research \cite{gimpel2023unlocking is a white paper which talks about unlocking the power of generative AI models and systems such as GPT-4 and ChatGPT as a publicly accessible system to improve efficiency in teaching and learning concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-075: This is an interesting thought process as in our current research our major focus is to leverage these models for technical analysis which are crucial to the design of Autonomous systems in vehicles.There is a goal in the Industry to explore on the concrete application possibilities of these ChatGPT type models.
elements.sent-2411-076: This paper is an attempt to make an increment towards the achievement of that goal.
elements.section-2411-003: Need for Gen AI in FTA analysis
elements.sent-2411-077: The entire FTA process currently involves a lot of manual analysis and the process can't be completed without taking help from multiple stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-078: It is heavily based on the documentation and recording of different failure modes in a natural language.
elements.sent-2411-079: As the complexity of the automotive SoC(System On Chip) grows, there will be a need to digitize these FTAs.
elements.sent-2411-080: Digitization of FTA is for processing and analyzing the FTA data to understand the potential hardware/software risks.
elements.sent-2411-081: To improve the quality of FTA, there will be a growing need to use the co-pilot who is trained with the prior/parent FTA data which can be further enhanced by the co-pilot\cite{lu2022safety by regenerating failure modes based on this empirical data.}
elements.sent-2411-082: In the context of AI, a co-pilot is an AI-powered virtual assistant designed to work alongside users, providing guidance, assistance, and even completing tasks on their behalf.
elements.sent-2411-083: It leverages large language models (LLMs) to understand and process information, and can be used in various domains and industries.
elements.sent-2411-084: For example, AI can analyze large datasets (e.g., historical failure data, and maintenance logs) to identify common failure modes and their causes.
elements.sent-2411-085: This can help in quickly generating an initial list of potential failures.
elements.sent-2411-086: AI algorithms can automate the probability calculation of various complex events by estimating the probability of occurrence of simple events.
elements.sent-2411-088: The paper discusses on various Large Language Models available in the Industry under Generative AI domain.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-089: Initial tests are proved on an open source LLM model which shows promising results.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-090: Experiments are conducted for various positive and negative scenarios for FTA generation using a GenAI model in the form of UML(Unified Modeling Language) code.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-091: PlantUML is explored in its entirety to generate FTA diagrams.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-092: Successful experiments open up scope for future work.
elements.sent-2411-093: Several standards documents and technical papers related to Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) have been published, outlining standard shapes and methodologies used in this analysis.\cite{backstrom2016effective Here are some key references: NRC NUREG–0492: This standard\cite{tankaas is specifically tailored for the nuclear power industry and outlines methodologies for FTA.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: It has been revised for aerospace applications by NASA.
elements.sent-2411-095: SAE ARP4761: This is a standard\cite{leveson2014comparison for civil aerospace, providing guidelines for performing FTA in the context of aviation safety.}
elements.sent-2411-096: MIL–HDBK–338: This military handbook\cite{moon1966use addresses FTA methodologies applicable to military systems, ensuring reliability and safety in defense applications.}
elements.sent-2411-097: IEC 61025: This international standard\cite{zornoza2018model is designed for cross-industry use and has been adopted as European Norm EN 61025, providing a comprehensive framework for conducting FTA across various sectors.}
elements.sent-2411-098: These documents collectively provide a robust foundation for understanding and implementing Fault Tree Analysis, including the standard shapes and logic used in constructing fault trees.
elements.sent-2411-099: For more specific standards related to transit and mobility data, the FTA Report No. 267\cite{FTA_Report discusses interoperability standards and specifications, which may also include graphical representations relevant to FTA methodologies in public transportation contexts.}
elements.subsection-2411-004: Most commonly used standard shapes in FTA
elements.sent-2411-100: In Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), several standard shapes and symbols are commonly used to represent events and logical relationships\ref{FTA_symbols .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Event Symbols Basic Event: Represents a failure or error in a system component (e.g., a switch stuck in the open position).. 2.
elements.sent-2411-103: External Event: Indicates an event that is normally expected to occur due to external factors but is not a fault.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-104: Undeveloped Event: Represents an event with insufficient information or one that is deemed inconsequential.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-105: Conditioning Event: Reflects conditions that affect the logic gates (e.g., mode of operation).
elements.sent-2411-106: OR Gate: The output occurs if any input occurs.
elements.sent-2411-107: AND Gate: The output occurs only if all inputs occur.
elements.sent-2411-108: Exclusive OR Gate: The output occurs if exactly one input occurs.
elements.sent-2411-109: Priority AND Gate: The output occurs if inputs occur in a specified sequence.
elements.sent-2411-110: Inhibit Gate: The output occurs if the input occurs under a specific enabling condition.
elements.sent-2411-111: Transfer Symbols: Used to connect the inputs and outputs of related fault trees, such as linking a subsystem's fault tree to its overall system fault tree.
elements.sent-2411-112: These symbols are essential for visually representing the relationships and events in an FTA, facilitating a clear understanding of system failures and their causes.
elements.subsection-2411-005: Benefits of using standardized shapes in FTA
elements.sent-2411-113: Using standardized shapes in Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) provides several key benefits:.
elements.sent-2411-114: The use of standardized event, gate, and transfer symbols creates a common visual language that makes fault trees easier to interpret and communicate.
elements.sent-2411-115: This clarity facilitates collaboration among diverse stakeholders like engineers, maintenance teams, and management.
elements.sent-2411-116: Adhering to standard shapes enables consistency in FTA modeling across an organization.
elements.sent-2411-117: This allows for better comparison of fault trees, identification of common failure patterns, and benchmarking of results.
elements.sent-2411-118: Major standards like NRC NUREG-0492, SAE ARP4761, and IEC 61025 specify the standard shapes to be used in FTA.
elements.sent-2411-119: Employing these shapes ensures alignment with industry best practices and facilitates compliance with regulations.
elements.sent-2411-120: The symbolic representations of events, gates, and transfers intuitively convey the relationships and logic in a fault tree.
elements.sent-2411-121: This makes the diagrams easier to interpret, especially for complex systems.
elements.sent-2411-122: FTA software tools are designed to work with the standard shapes.
elements.sent-2411-123: Using these shapes ensures compatibility and enables the use of automated analysis features provided by the software.
elements.sent-2411-124: In summary, standardized shapes are essential for creating clear, consistent, and industry-aligned fault trees that can be readily interpreted and analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-125: They form a critical component of effective FTA practice.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Leverage these standardized shapes to help train individuals or models to do an FTA on a component described
elements.sent-2411-126: To leverage standardized shapes in Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) for training purposes, the following strategies can be employed: 1.
elements.sent-2411-127: Structured Training Modules Develop training modules that focus on the specific standardized shapes used in FTA, such as basic events, gates (AND, OR, etc.), and transfer symbols.
elements.sent-2411-128: Each module can include: Visual Aids: Use diagrams to illustrate each shape and its meaning.
elements.sent-2411-129: Examples: Provide real-world examples of fault trees that utilize these shapes effectively.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-130: Interactive Learning Tools Utilize software tools that allow trainees to create fault trees using standardized shapes.
elements.sent-2411-131: This hands-on approach can include: Simulation Exercises: Create scenarios where trainees must identify potential failures and construct a fault tree using the correct shapes.
elements.sent-2411-132: Feedback Mechanisms: Implement systems for providing immediate feedback on the accuracy of the fault tree diagrams created by trainees.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-133: Collaborative Workshops Organize workshops that encourage collaboration among participants from different disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-134: This can enhance understanding through: Group Activities: Facilitate group exercises where participants work together to analyze a component and construct a fault tree.
elements.sent-2411-135: Cross-Disciplinary Perspectives: Involve engineers, maintenance personnel, and safety experts to provide diverse insights on the component being analyzed.. 4.
elements.sent-2411-136: Case Studies and Best Practices Incorporate case studies that highlight successful applications of FTA in various industries.
elements.sent-2411-137: Focus on: Lessons Learned: Discuss how standardized shapes helped identify critical failures and improve system reliability.
elements.sent-2411-138: Regulatory Compliance: Emphasize how using standardized shapes aligns with industry regulations and best practices, enhancing safety and compliance efforts.. 5.
elements.sent-2411-139: Continuous Improvement and Updates Encourage ongoing learning by: Regularly Updating Training Materials: As new standards or shapes emerge, ensure training content reflects the most current practices.
elements.sent-2411-140: Feedback Loops: Collect feedback from trainees to continuously improve the training process and materials.
elements.sent-2411-141: By implementing these strategies, organizations can effectively train personnel in FTA using standardized shapes, ultimately leading to better risk management and system reliability.
elements.section-2411-005: Using LLMs to produce basic FTA models
elements.sent-2411-142: We used several publicly available LLMs like OpenAI's ChatGPT\cite{ChatGPT , Google's Gemini\cite{Gemini and Perplexity\cite{Perplexity to train them to generate desired Fault Tree analysis through Prompt Engineering.}}}

elements.subsection-2411-007: Major challenges faced in this experiment
elements.sent-2411-168: We realized that although we can use these standardized LLMs to get FTAs, we cannot get the standardized flowchart shapes.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-169: There is a need to build an example LLM model that recognizes these shapes.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-170: Need to evaluate possibility of different modeling techniques and sync them with Perplexity to get FTAs.
elements.sent-2411-171: In order to overcome above challenges, we took the aid of PlantUML\cite{lund2022towards .}
elements.sent-2411-172: PlantUML is a powerful tool that allows you to create various types of diagrams using a simple text-based syntax.
elements.sent-2411-173: This means you can define diagrams using plain text, which can be easily integrated into your documentation or source code.
elements.sent-2411-174: The ease with which this can be integrated in our VS code environment was another major factor for us to choose this line of research for next experiments.

elements.sent-2411-182: Before fixing upon our preferred way to get FTA, we evaluated potential tools to best fit our usecase.
elements.sent-2411-183: This tool\cite{FTA_Tool was available open source and an Industry standard used in automotive FTA designs.}
elements.sent-2411-184: Based upon our analysis, this tool was found to be specific to particular ECU(Electronic Control Unit) use cases and not fit to be used with our model.
elements.sent-2411-185: This tool\cite{Mermaid_Tool is a popular to generate sequence diagrams in software applications.}
elements.sent-2411-186: However as we needed more top-down analysis for our FTA and less sequences , we decided not to use Mermaid.
elements.sent-2411-187: This is a web-based tool\cite{yUML_Tool that allows you to create UML diagrams using a simple text syntax.}
elements.sent-2411-188: However we found we could use the simple tax which yUML uses but have it in-built as an extension in our VS code.
elements.sent-2411-189: These tools\cite{nomnoml_Tool}\cite{kroki_Tool are good for class diagrams and Kroki in particular is an API which uses backend to generate diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-190: As our usecase was not necessary software based, we decided not to use these approaches.
elements.sent-2411-191: We decided to use syntax similar to yUML but plug it in as an extension with VS code on our system to generate and verify UML diagrams.
elements.subsection-2411-010: PlantUML modified FTA to fix errors
elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Prompt 1:give a Plant UML code to generate an FTA for lidar sensor in for level 4 autonomy in all weather conditions
elements.lstlisting-2411-002: [caption{Code to generate Perplexity generated FTA after all modifications using PlantUML}, label=lst:PlantUMLmodifiedFTA] @startuml LIDAR Sensor Failure FTA skinparam packageStyle rectangle skinparam linetype ortho rectangle "LIDAR Sensor Failure" as TopEvent package "Hardware" { rectangle "Emitter" as Emitter rectangle "Detector" as Detector rectangle "Scanner" as Scanner } package "Software" { rectangle "Processing" as Processing } package "Environmental" { rectangle "Interference" as Interference rectangle "Obstruction" as Obstruction } TopEvent -- Hardware TopEvent -- Software TopEvent -- Environmental Hardware -- Emitter Hardware -- Detector Hardware -- Scanner Software -- Processing Environmental -- Interference Environmental -- Obstruction rectangle "A: Laser diode degradation" as A rectangle "B: Power supply issues" as B rectangle "C: Overheating" as C Emitter -- A Emitter -- B Emitter -- C rectangle "D: Photodiode damage" as D rectangle "E: Amplifier malfunction" as E rectangle "F: Electrical connection issues" as F Detector -- D Detector -- E Detector -- F rectangle "G: Motor breakdown" as G rectangle "H: Mirror/prism damage" as H rectangle "I: Bearing wear" as I Scanner -- G Scanner -- H Scanner -- I rectangle "J: Algorithm bugs" as J rectangle "K: Insufficient processing power" as K Processing -- J Processing -- K rectangle "L: Adverse weather" as L rectangle "M: Direct sunlight glare" as M rectangle "N: Reflective surfaces" as N Interference -- L Interference -- M Interference -- N rectangle "O: Debris accumulation" as O rectangle "P: Impact damage" as P rectangle "Q: Misalignment from vibration" as Q Obstruction -- O Obstruction -- P Obstruction -- Q note bottom of TopEvent All connections represent OR gates Any lower-level event can cause the higher-level failure end note @enduml
elements.sent-2411-193: Explanation given by Perplexity in words: Top Event: The diagram starts with the top event "LIDAR Sensor Failure in Level 4 Autonomy".
elements.sent-2411-194: This represents the overall system failure we're analyzing.
elements.sent-2411-195: Main Categories: The top event is connected to four main categories of failure through an OR gate, meaning that any of these categories can lead to the top event: a) Hardware Failure b) Software Failure c) Environmental Factors d) Integration Issues Hardware Failure: This category includes physical component failures, such as: Laser emitter degradation Detector malfunction Scanning mechanism failure Power supply issues Software Failure: This covers issues related to data processing and algorithms: Algorithm bugs Insufficient processing power Object detection/classification errors Prediction algorithm failure Environmental Factors: These are external conditions that can affect LIDAR performance: Adverse weather (rain, snow, fog) Direct sunlight glare Reflective surfaces Debris accumulation Integration Issues: This category addresses problems with how the LIDAR system interacts with other vehicle systems: Sensor fusion errors Calibration drift Data throughput limitations V2X communication failure Logical Relationships: All connections in this diagram use OR gates, indicating that any single failure mode within a category can potentially lead to the higher-level failure.
elements.sent-2411-196: Level 4 Autonomy Context: A note in the diagram emphasizes that Level 4 autonomy requires enhanced sensor suites and processing capabilities to handle complex urban environments and all-weather conditions.
elements.sent-2411-197: This reflects the increased demands on LIDAR systems in highly autonomous vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-198: Based on the explanation given by Perplexity, one can conclude that by providing just a high level statement, the LLM is capable to break down the user's requirements and perform complete top-down analysis.
elements.sent-2411-199: This shows that on the technical front , the coverage is good.
elements.sent-2411-200: We now have to work on the Representation front.
elements.sent-2411-201: For this, we first start by correcting the errors generated while previewing the PlantUML model.
elements.sent-2411-202: Error1: Syntax Error? (@ Diagram Line 26, File Line 27) "HardwareOR -- rectangle "Detector malfunction"" (Error) We wanted to promt engineer Perplexity to debug errors.
elements.sent-2411-203: So we entered the errors as it is in the prompts.
elements.sent-2411-204: Following were the set of error prompts which Perplexity went on learning to modify its code to get a proper diagram: 1.
elements.sent-2411-205: Syntax Error? (@ Diagram Line 25, File Line 26) "HardwareAND -down-> rectangle "Power supply issues"" (Error) 4.
elements.sent-2411-206: Syntax Error? (@ Diagram Line 5, File Line 6) " arc" (Error) 6.
elements.sent-2411-207: Syntax Error? (@ Diagram Line 12, File Line 13) \text{AND\_GATE(HardwareAND) (Error) 7.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Syntax Error? (@ Diagram Line 9, File Line 10) "diamond HardwareAND" (Error) We used the code generated by Perplexity, not shown in our paper currently as its for seven sets of errors and erroneous , in our VS code environment and copied the errors received in the above sequence into Perplexity.
elements.subsubsection-2411-014: Final generated PlantUML code and diagram
elements.lstlisting-2411-003: [caption{Code to generate Perplexity generated FTA after resolution of all errors using PlantUML}, label=lst:PlantUMLFinalFTA] @startuml LIDAR Sensor Failure FTA skinparam rectangle { roundCorner 25 } rectangle "LIDAR Sensor Failure" as TopEvent circle MainOR circle HardwareAND circle SoftwareOR circle EnvironmentalAND rectangle "Hardware Failure" as HardwareFailure rectangle "Software Failure" as SoftwareFailure rectangle "Environmental Factors" as EnvironmentalFactors TopEvent -down-> MainOR : OR MainOR -down-> HardwareFailure MainOR -down-> SoftwareFailure MainOR -down-> EnvironmentalFactors HardwareFailure -down-> HardwareAND : AND HardwareAND -down-> (Laser emitter degradation) HardwareAND -down-> (Power supply issues) SoftwareFailure -down-> SoftwareOR : OR SoftwareOR -down-> (Algorithm bugs) SoftwareOR -down-> (Insufficient processing power) EnvironmentalFactors -down-> EnvironmentalAND : AND EnvironmentalAND -down-> (Adverse weather) EnvironmentalAND -down-> (Direct sunlight glare) note right of TopEvent Circles labeled 'OR' represent OR gates Circles labeled 'AND' represent AND gates end note @enduml
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{tabular}{c|c} \multicolumn{2}{c}{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth,height=3.0in]{Final1.PNG}} \end{tabular} \caption{Plant UML generated FTA after resolution of all errors} \label{Plant UML generated FTA after resolution of all errors}
elements.sent-2411-209: Code snippet\ref{lst:PlantUMLFinalFTA and the figure\ref{Plant UML generated FTA after resolution of all errors are the final code blocks and FTA respectively which meet our initial requirement to get a proper breakdown of all critical events necessary to perform Fault analysis of a Lidar Sensor Failure with appropriate GATES, simplified and representation as per Industry standards.}}
elements.sent-2411-210: We tried to further enhance the \ref{lst:PlantUMLperformanceFTA model by adding additional possible events for Lidar failure.}
elements.sent-2411-211: It understood the task and generated the UML code for the PlantUML model \ref{lst:PlantUMLperformanceFTA shown below:.}
elements.lstlisting-2411-004: [caption{Code to generate Perplexity generated FTA after adding additional performance degradation factor using PlantUML}, label=lst:PlantUMLperformanceFTA] @startuml LIDAR Sensor Failure or Degradation in Level 4 Autonomy skinparam rectangle { roundCorner 25 } rectangle "LIDAR Sensor Failure or Significant Degradation\nin Level 4 Autonomy" as TopEvent rectangle "Hardware Failure" as HardwareFailure rectangle "Software Failure" as SoftwareFailure rectangle "Environmental Factors" as EnvironmentalFactors rectangle "Integration Issues" as IntegrationIssues rectangle "Performance Degradation" as PerformanceDegradation TopEvent --> HardwareFailure TopEvent --> SoftwareFailure TopEvent --> EnvironmentalFactors TopEvent --> IntegrationIssues TopEvent --> PerformanceDegradation HardwareFailure --> "Laser emitter degradation" HardwareFailure --> "Detector malfunction" HardwareFailure --> "Scanning mechanism failure" HardwareFailure --> "Power supply issues" SoftwareFailure --> "Algorithm bugs" SoftwareFailure --> "Insufficient processing power" SoftwareFailure --> "Object detection/classification errors" SoftwareFailure --> "Prediction algorithm failure" EnvironmentalFactors --> "Adverse weather (rain, snow, fog)" EnvironmentalFactors --> "Direct sunlight glare" EnvironmentalFactors --> "Reflective surfaces" EnvironmentalFactors --> "Debris accumulation" IntegrationIssues --> "Sensor fusion errors" IntegrationIssues --> "Calibration drift" IntegrationIssues --> "Data throughput limitations" IntegrationIssues --> "V2X communication failure" PerformanceDegradation --> "Range reduction" PerformanceDegradation --> "Resolution decrease" PerformanceDegradation --> "Increased noise in point cloud" PerformanceDegradation --> "Slower scan rate" note right of TopEvent Level 4 autonomy requires enhanced sensor suites and processing capabilities to handle complex urban environments and all-weather conditions. Some failures require multiple conditions (AND gates). end note @enduml
elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{turn}{90} \includegraphics[width=1.2\textwidth]{perform_degrade_1.PNG} \end{turn} \caption{Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events left part of flowchart} \label{fig:Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events left part of flowchart}
elements.figure-2411-015: \begin{turn}{90} \includegraphics[width=1.2\textwidth]{perform_degrade_2.PNG} \end{turn} \caption{Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events right part of flowchart} \label{fig:Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events right part of flowchart}
elements.sent-2411-212: Code snippet\ref{lst:PlantUMLperformanceFTA and the figure\ref{fig:Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events left part of flowchart and \ref{fig:Plant UML generated FTA with performance degradation added to events right part of flowchart are the final code blocks and FTA diagram respectively which meet our requirements to get an additional critical event added to perform Fault analysis of a Lidar Sensor Failure.}}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Initially the model was not using standard FTA shapes.
elements.sent-2411-214: When prompted to use AND/OR gates, the model ran into errors as PlantUML previewer could not understand complicated FTA shapes.
elements.sent-2411-215: Upon asking Perplexity itself to resolve errors, the model kind of back traced its errors to find an intermediate solution which used standard shapes and performs FTA.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-216: The model performance did not degrade and it provided the necessary UML code when we added additional events for FTA cause.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-217: The model understands the usage of AND/OR gates.
elements.sent-2411-218: Based on the learnings from training the LLM model, we conclude that the Perplexity model very well understands the technical aspects on the derivation of FTA for a particular failure.
elements.sent-2411-219: The model however needs sufficient training to get it in the right format with gates and ensure correctness as it modifies the UML code.
elements.sent-2411-220: As a next step, we will be using this for FMEA analysis in our next paper.
elements.sent-2411-221: Current paper majorly focuses on one particular usecase for Lidar failure.
elements.sent-2411-222: There is scope for lot of work to improvise on this by using various FTA usecases for sensors like cameras, Radars and their respective faults in the autonomous vehicle field.
elements.sent-2411-223: For our analysis, we focused on Perplexity and other open source LLM models.
elements.sent-2411-224: As a future scope , one can generate an LLM model specifically which does FTA for Autonomous features and the research in this paper can serve as ground work for the same.
elements.sent-2411-225: Also, once the new LLM model has been trained to output the entire FTA diagram with the relevant logic representation and blocks, PlantUML would be completely replaced with the LLM itself.
elements.sent-2411-226: This should enable performing the entire FTA using LLM with a single or fewer prompts.
elements.sent-2411-227: This capability of FTA generation from the LLM could then be used in different applications and use cases such as healthcare, aerospace, and others.
elements.sent-2411-228: She received her Bachelor's degree in Electronics and Communication Engineering from the Visvesvaraya Technological University, Karnataka, India, in 2014.
elements.sent-2411-229: Her work involves middleware topics for embedded development of autonomous driving stack, automotive diagnostics, systems engineering and functional safety.
elements.sent-2411-230: She is an active volunteer with IEEE region 4 and takes part in activities of Women In Engineering (WIE) groups in the region.
elements.sent-2411-231: She is part of the committee for senior member evaluation at IEEE for 2024 and has been a proctor for IEEExtreme 24 hour coding competition.
elements.sent-2411-232: She actively participates in several industry level standards discussions within IEEE and SAE organizations.
elements.sent-2411-233: She is also the working group chair for the IEEE Vehicular Technology Society standards discussions.
elements.sent-2411-234: She received her Masters in Electrical Engineering Systems from the University of Michigan, Ann Arbor in 2014.
elements.sent-2411-235: Prior to that, she received her Bachelors in Electronics and Communications Engineering from Andhra University College of Engineering, Andhra Pradesh, India.
elements.sent-2411-236: Her current work focuses on Systems and Safety research and development for Level 2,3 and 4 Autonomous vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-237: She has over 10 years of experience in the automotive industry.
elements.sent-2411-238: She has 7 years of experience in Automotive Safety working at renowned Organizations.
elements.sent-2411-239: She graduated from Michigan Technological University in May 2021 with a Masters in Electrical Engineering.
elements.sent-2411-240: She was a CHEB member of Society of Women Engineers at Michigan Tech.
elements.sent-2411-241: Her work involves supporting stakeholder requirement elicitation for SoC, its analysis and modelling various simulated use-cases in closed loop.. \end{IEEEbiography.}
